variant_label: Oil production & trade — Iranian oil share of free world crude oil supply
notes: Estimated share of the free world's crude oil supply accounted for by Iranian production before the shutdown. [cite: FRUS 1952-1954, Iran, 1951-1954, Document 39: Intelligence Memorandum Prepared in the Office of Current Intelligence, CIA (SIC No. 9750), "Effects of Closing Down the Iranian Oil Industry", Washington, July 11, 1951, PDF p. 153 (printed p. 116)]

variant_label: Oil production & trade — Iranian oil share of free world refined products supply
notes: Estimated share of free world refined petroleum products supply from Iran. [cite: FRUS 1952-1954, Iran, 1951-1954, Document 39: Intelligence Memorandum Prepared in the Office of Current Intelligence, CIA (SIC No. 9750), "Effects of Closing Down the Iranian Oil Industry", Washington, July 11, 1951, PDF p. 153 (printed p. 116)]

variant_label: Oil production & trade — Continental Western Europe refined-products dependence on Iran
notes: Share of Continental Western Europe's refined products secured from Iran. [cite: FRUS 1952-1954, Iran, 1951-1954, Document 39: Intelligence Memorandum Prepared in the Office of Current Intelligence, CIA (SIC No. 9750), "Effects of Closing Down the Iranian Oil Industry", Washington, July 11, 1951, PDF p. 153 (printed p. 116)]

variant_label: Oil production & trade — Continental Western Europe crude-oil dependence on Iran
notes: Share of Continental Western Europe's crude oil secured from Iran. [cite: FRUS 1952-1954, Iran, 1951-1954, Document 39: Intelligence Memorandum Prepared in the Office of Current Intelligence, CIA (SIC No. 9750), "Effects of Closing Down the Iranian Oil Industry", Washington, July 11, 1951, PDF p. 153 (printed p. 116)]

variant_label: Oil production & trade — UK domestic needs supplied by AIOC
notes: Share of UK domestic petroleum needs supplied by the Anglo-Iranian Oil Company. [cite: FRUS 1952-1954, Iran, 1951-1954, Document 39: Intelligence Memorandum Prepared in the Office of Current Intelligence, CIA (SIC No. 9750), "Effects of Closing Down the Iranian Oil Industry", Washington, July 11, 1951, PDF p. 153 (printed p. 116)]

variant_label: Oil production & trade — South Asia oil dependence on Iran
notes: "Close to 70%" of South Asia's oil and oil products supplied by Iran. [cite: FRUS 1952-1954, Iran, 1951-1954, Document 39: Intelligence Memorandum Prepared in the Office of Current Intelligence, CIA (SIC No. 9750), "Effects of Closing Down the Iranian Oil Industry", Washington, July 11, 1951, PDF p. 153 (printed p. 116)]

variant_label: Oil production & trade — Abadan refinery lost output if closed
notes: Loss of refined products from Abadan estimated at 550,000 barrels (~78,570 metric tons) per day -- the most serious world-supply repercussion of a shutdown. [cite: FRUS 1952-1954, Iran, 1951-1954, Document 39: Intelligence Memorandum Prepared in the Office of Current Intelligence, CIA (SIC No. 9750), "Effects of Closing Down the Iranian Oil Industry", Washington, July 11, 1951, PDF p. 153 (printed p. 116)]

variant_label: Oil production & trade — Haifa refinery utilization
notes: Haifa refinery (Israel) operating at ~25% of its 85,000 barrels (~12,140 metric tons)/day capacity at the time of the report. [cite: FRUS 1952-1954, Iran, 1951-1954, Document 39: Intelligence Memorandum Prepared in the Office of Current Intelligence, CIA (SIC No. 9750), "Effects of Closing Down the Iranian Oil Industry", Washington, July 11, 1951, PDF p. 153 (printed p. 116)]

variant_label: Oil production & trade — Soviet Orbit potential first-year petroleum imports from Iran
notes: Estimated volume the Soviet Orbit could import and utilize from Iran during the first year of substantial shipments (assuming USSR gains unlimited access). [cite: FRUS 1952-1954, Iran, 1951-1954, Document 45: Study Prepared in the Office of Research and Reports, CIA, "The Importance and Availability of Iranian Oil to the USSR Under Peacetime Conditions", Washington, September 22, 1951, PDF p. 171 (printed p. 134)]

variant_label: Oil production & trade — Of which, movable by sea tanker to Soviet Far East/China
notes: Portion of the 2.9 million ton estimate that could move by tanker to the Soviet Far East and Communist China. [cite: FRUS 1952-1954, Iran, 1951-1954, Document 45: Study Prepared in the Office of Research and Reports, CIA, "The Importance and Availability of Iranian Oil to the USSR Under Peacetime Conditions", Washington, September 22, 1951, PDF p. 171 (printed p. 134)]

variant_label: Oil production & trade — Of which, movable by rail Abadan-Bandar Shah then Caspian tanker
notes: Remaining portion shippable by rail from Abadan to Bandar Shah, then by tanker to Caspian Sea ports. [cite: FRUS 1952-1954, Iran, 1951-1954, Document 45: Study Prepared in the Office of Research and Reports, CIA, "The Importance and Availability of Iranian Oil to the USSR Under Peacetime Conditions", Washington, September 22, 1951, PDF p. 171 (printed p. 134)]

variant_label: Oil production & trade — Estimated Soviet Orbit imports as share of 1950 Abadan production
notes: The estimated first-year Soviet Orbit import volume equals only 12% of the Abadan refinery's 1950 production level. [cite: FRUS 1952-1954, Iran, 1951-1954, Document 45: Study Prepared in the Office of Research and Reports, CIA, "The Importance and Availability of Iranian Oil to the USSR Under Peacetime Conditions", Washington, September 22, 1951, PDF p. 171 (printed p. 134)]

variant_label: Oil production & trade — IBRD oil-sale settlement proposal: discount off world price
notes: World Bank (IBRD) proposal for resolving the oil dispute: oil sold at a 33.5% discount internationally through established British channels. [cite: FRUS 1952-1954, Iran, 1951-1954, Document 61: Despatch From the Station in Iran to the Central Intelligence Agency, Tehran, January 10, 1952, PDF p. 209 (printed p. 172)]

variant_label: Oil production & trade — IBRD proposal: Bank/Iran government revenue split
notes: Under the IBRD proposal the Bank and the Iranian Government would split the remaining revenues on a 50-50 basis after the discount, with the Bank's share funding Abadan operating costs. [cite: FRUS 1952-1954, Iran, 1951-1954, Document 61: Despatch From the Station in Iran to the Central Intelligence Agency, Tehran, January 10, 1952, PDF p. 209 (printed p. 172)]

variant_label: Oil production & trade — Independent tanker capacity available to move Iranian oil
notes: Department experts concluded sufficient independent tankers existed to move 100,000 to 150,000 barrels per day of Iranian oil despite the informal blockade. [cite: FRUS 1952-1954, Iran, 1951-1954, Document 121: Memorandum From the Assistant Secretary of State for Near Eastern, South Asian and African Affairs (footnote referencing the July 31, 1952 Aide-Memoire, printed as Document 194 in the companion FRUS vol. X), Washington, September 10, 1952 (Aide-Memoire itself dated July 31, 1952), PDF p. 375 (printed p. 338)]

variant_label: Oil production & trade — Discount offered by Iran to attract oil buyers
notes: Despite Iran's willingness to extend a 50 percent discount off world prices (and easing tanker supply), sales remained minimal because major companies avoided clashing with AIOC. [cite: FRUS 1952-1954, Iran, 1951-1954, Document 259: Draft National Intelligence Estimate, Washington, August 12, 1953, PDF p. 696 (printed p. 659)]

variant_label: Oil production & trade — Abadan refinery restoration cost estimate
notes: An expenditure variously estimated from $30,000,000 to $60,000,000 would be required to place the Abadan refinery back into partial operation after its post-1951 disrepair. [cite: FRUS 1952-1954, Iran, 1951-1954, Document 347: National Intelligence Estimate, Washington, November 16, 1953, PDF p. 878 (printed p. 841)]

variant_label: Oil production & trade — Abadan refinery historical throughput
notes: The Abadan refinery, the world's largest, formerly had a through-put in excess of 500,000 barrels per day before the 1951 shutdown. [cite: FRUS 1952-1954, Iran, 1951-1954, Document 347: National Intelligence Estimate, Washington, November 16, 1953, PDF p. 878 (printed p. 841)]

variant_label: Oil production & trade — Abadan refinery reduced target throughput (restart plan)
notes: Even a reduced through-put target of 300,000 barrels per day would present a severe marketing/distribution problem given the post-war shift toward refining near consumption points. [cite: FRUS 1952-1954, Iran, 1951-1954, Document 347: National Intelligence Estimate, Washington, November 16, 1953, PDF p. 878 (printed p. 841)]

variant_label: Oil production & trade — Iranian crude production reached prior to 1951 shutdown
notes: Iranian crude production reached about 650,000 barrels per day prior to the 1951 shutdown. [cite: FRUS 1952-1954, Iran, 1951-1954, Document 347: National Intelligence Estimate, Washington, November 16, 1953, PDF p. 878 (printed p. 841)]

variant_label: Oil production & trade — Middle East share of Eastern Hemisphere oil refining
notes: In 1945, 82 percent of all oil refined in the Eastern Hemisphere was processed in the Middle East. [cite: FRUS 1952-1954, Iran, 1951-1954, Document 347: National Intelligence Estimate, Washington, November 16, 1953, PDF p. 878 (printed p. 841)]

variant_label: Oil production & trade — Middle East share of Eastern Hemisphere oil refining (1953)
notes: By 1953 the Middle East's share of Eastern Hemisphere refining had declined to 19 percent, as refining relocated toward consumption points. [cite: FRUS 1952-1954, Iran, 1951-1954, Document 347: National Intelligence Estimate, Washington, November 16, 1953, PDF p. 878 (printed p. 841)]

variant_label: Oil production & trade — European share of Eastern Hemisphere oil refining
notes: In 1945, European refineries processed only 6 percent of Eastern Hemisphere refining volume. [cite: FRUS 1952-1954, Iran, 1951-1954, Document 347: National Intelligence Estimate, Washington, November 16, 1953, PDF p. 878 (printed p. 841)]

variant_label: Oil production & trade — European share of Eastern Hemisphere oil refining (1953)
notes: By 1953 Europe's share had risen to 63 percent of Eastern Hemisphere refining volume. [cite: FRUS 1952-1954, Iran, 1951-1954, Document 347: National Intelligence Estimate, Washington, November 16, 1953, PDF p. 878 (printed p. 841)]

variant_label: Oil production & trade — Middle East net oil income per barrel (royalties + taxes)
notes: Existing net income (royalties and taxes) received by Middle Eastern oil-producing countries was approximately 70 to 80 cents per barrel. [cite: FRUS 1952-1954, Iran, 1951-1954, Document 347: National Intelligence Estimate, Washington, November 16, 1953, PDF p. 878 (printed p. 841)]

variant_label: Oil production & trade — Saudi Arabia projected 1953 net oil income
notes: Saudi Arabia was projected to receive net income in excess of $200 million for the year 1953 -- offered as a comparator for what Iran might obtain from a settlement. [cite: FRUS 1952-1954, Iran, 1951-1954, Document 347: National Intelligence Estimate, Washington, November 16, 1953, PDF p. 878 (printed p. 841)]

variant_label: Oil production & trade — Projected European dependence on Middle East for peacetime crude oil by 1975
notes: 1953-era projection: by 1975 Europe will be dependent upon the Middle East for at least 90% of its peacetime crude oil. [cite: FRUS 1952-1954, Iran, 1951-1954, Document 347: National Intelligence Estimate, Washington, November 16, 1953, PDF p. 878 (printed p. 841)]

variant_label: Oil production & trade — Projected European Middle East crude oil imports by 1975
notes: Corresponding 1953-era projection of the volume: Europe would require imports of 3.7 million barrels/day of Middle East crude by 1975. [cite: FRUS 1952-1954, Iran, 1951-1954, Document 347: National Intelligence Estimate, Washington, November 16, 1953, PDF p. 878 (printed p. 841)]

variant_label: Oil production & trade — Projected US peacetime imports of Middle East crude oil by 1975
notes: 1953-era projection: the United States would require peacetime imports of 1.2 million barrels/day of Middle East crude oil by 1975. [cite: FRUS 1952-1954, Iran, 1951-1954, Document 347: National Intelligence Estimate, Washington, November 16, 1953, PDF p. 878 (printed p. 841)]

variant_label: Oil production & trade — Projected Middle East share of total US peacetime oil requirement by 1975
notes: The projected 1.2 million b/d import figure was estimated at 8.8% of total US peacetime oil requirement in 1975. [cite: FRUS 1952-1954, Iran, 1951-1954, Document 347: National Intelligence Estimate, Washington, November 16, 1953, PDF p. 878 (printed p. 841)]
